blocks.text: Mark Villegas, Chief Financial Officer, {{fact:f:E-2021-001.client}}
Re: Back-Office Automation for Garcia-Werner
Dear Mr. Villegas,

This letter confirms the engagement and records the terms we will work under. What we have agreed to settle is one question: which of the routine back-office tasks that {{fact:f:E-2021-001.client}} now performs by hand can be handled by software instead, and of those, which are worth changing and in what order. The scope, the people, the fee, and the standard terms that accompany our work follow, so that both sides begin from a single reading of what we have undertaken.
What the engagement covers

The engagement examines the transactional back-office functions that run under your office: the processing of payables and receivables, routine reconciliations, expense and payroll administration, the procurement paperwork, and the handoffs between them. We will document how each of these is performed today, measure the volume and effort it consumes, and pick out the steps that are repetitive and rule-based enough to be carried by software. From that we will produce a ranked set of automation opportunities, each with an estimate of the effort to put it in place and the time or cost it would return, and a recommended sequence for taking them on. We will not put a change forward without showing the working behind it, and where the underlying records are thin we will say so rather than present an estimate as a measurement.

Two matters sit outside this letter. We are not being retained to build or install the automation itself; our work ends with a tested set of recommendations and a sequence that your teams, or an implementation partner of your choosing, can act on. And we are not conducting a procurement: where a recommendation points to a category of software, we will describe the category and what it must do, but the selection and negotiation of a particular product is a separate matter we would take up under a separate letter if you wished.
How we will go about it

The work runs in two parts over roughly four months. In the first we establish an accurate current-state picture: how the back-office processes actually run, what volumes pass through them, and where time and effort accumulate. This rests on your operating records and on time with the people who do the work, and it depends on reasonable access to both. Our associates will send a first, specific data request in the opening days, and we would ask that one person on your side be named to route those requests, so that neither team loses time finding the right contact. In the second part we take the opportunities the diagnosis exposes, weigh them against effort and return, and set out a target design and a transition outline. We will test each recommendation against the volumes you actually carry before it reaches you.
Who will do the work

Pamela Miller and Matthew Parrish, both Research Associates of the firm, will carry the analysis and prepare the written record you receive between our formal reviews. I will stay close to the engagement and will read everything that leaves this office under the firm's name. You should expect working sessions with your team, minutes of those sessions, status reports written for you at regular points, and a briefing to your wider group once the analysis is firm enough to defend. Your point of contact on anything touching the substance of the work will be Pamela Miller; anything touching the terms of this letter comes to me.
Fee and start date

Our fee for the engagement is a fixed fee of {{fact:f:E-2021-001.fee}}, which covers the whole of the work described above. It is not a rate billed against hours, and it will not change unless the scope itself changes and we record that change in writing. We propose to begin on {{fact:f:E-2021-001.start}}, and the engagement is planned to run about four months from that point, with the pace of the later weeks depending in part on how quickly the operating records can be made available to us.
Fees and payment

The fixed fee of {{fact:f:E-2021-001.fee}} will be invoiced in equal monthly installments across the term, each invoice payable within thirty days of its date. Reasonable travel and out-of-pocket expenses incurred in the course of the work are billed at cost, itemized on the relevant invoice, and are not part of the fixed fee. We will not commit any single expense above a modest threshold without your agreement in advance.
Term and termination

The engagement begins on the date named above and continues until the work is complete or the parties agree in writing to end it. Either party may terminate on thirty days' written notice. If the engagement ends early, the fixed fee is prorated to the work performed to the date of termination, and expenses properly incurred to that date remain payable. Any work product prepared before termination is available to you on the same terms as work delivered at completion.
Confidentiality

Each party will treat as confidential the non-public information it receives from the other in the course of the work, will use that information only for the purposes of the engagement, and will not disclose it to a third party without the other's consent, except where disclosure is required by law. The obligation outlasts the engagement. We will limit access to your information to the members of our team who need it for the work, and we will return or destroy it at your request when the engagement closes.
Limitation of liability

Our advice is prepared for the use of {{fact:f:E-2021-001.client}} in connection with this engagement and should not be relied upon by any other party or for any other purpose. Our aggregate liability arising out of or in connection with the engagement, whether in contract, in tort, or otherwise, is limited to the fees paid to us under this letter, except in the case of our fraud or willful misconduct. The decisions taken on the strength of our work remain yours to make.
Governing law

This letter and the engagement it describes are governed by the law of the state in which the firm maintains its principal office, and any dispute arising under it will be resolved in the courts of that state. If any provision of this letter is found unenforceable, the rest of it stands.

If these terms are acceptable, please countersign below and return one copy to us. The signed letter will stand as our agreement, and we will make arrangements to begin on the date named above. Should any provision here read as unclear, raise it with me before the work starts and we will settle it plainly.
Yours sincerely,
Kickoff notes: the Garcia-Werner back-office automation work

items: To: Matthew Parrish
From: Pamela Miller
Re: how we will run the opening weeks

blocks.text: This is a short note on how we will run the first weeks of the back-office automation work. The engagement began on {{fact:f:E-2021-001.start}} and runs about four months. What we have been asked to do is plain enough to state in a sentence: look at the routine back-office tasks {{fact:f:E-2021-001.client}} handles by hand today, work out which of them software could take over, and come back with a ranked list of the changes worth making and the order to make them in. The work sits mostly in the finance office, which is why the CFO is our sponsor. Everything we recommend later stands on the current-state picture we build now, so the early weeks go to getting that picture right and agreed before we lean on it.
What the work breaks into

items: Current-state map. Write down how each back-office process actually runs today, step by step, including where a task waits and where it passes from one person or team to another. I will note the source for every step, so anyone reading it later can see where it came from.

Volume and effort baseline. Attach the volumes and the time each process takes, drawn from the client's own records rather than from our guesses, so the diagnosis rests on numbers Garcia-Werner recognizes as its own.
Automation candidates. From the map and the baseline, pull out the steps that are repetitive and rule-bound enough to automate, and describe what each one would take.
Ranking and sequence. Weigh the candidates against the effort to do them and the time or cost they would return, and put them in an order that front-loads the clear wins.

blocks.text: A word on the baseline, since everything hangs on it. A current-state figure is only as good as the record behind it. Where a number comes from a system report, I will mark it as solid; where it rests on a single month or on a manager's recollection, I will flag it as provisional and hold it apart until the client confirms it. What we are after is a picture both sides trust, so any figure nobody on their side will stand behind gets confirmed or dropped before it goes into the baseline.

On who does what: you will pull and check the client's operating data as it comes in, and I will keep the running map and the written record we hand back. Anything that goes to the client gets a read here before it leaves, the same as on any engagement.
Getting started

items: I will send the first data request in the opening days, scoped to process volumes, cycle times, and the effort each process takes.
Confirm with Mark Villegas, the client's Chief Financial Officer, a single person on their side to route data and access requests to.
Set the first working session once the opening records are in hand.
You and I to agree the list of processes we will map, so we are not each working from a different one.

blocks.text: On cadence: working sessions will produce written minutes, and the client will get a status report from us at regular points. Between those, most of what passes to the client will be short notes about data and scheduling. If anything in the framing above looks wrong to you, flag it before we take it to their team and I will fix it first.
Mark Villegas,

Thanks for the payables and reconciliation records that came through last week. Matthew Parrish and I have started mapping how the back-office work actually moves through {{fact:f:E-2021-001.client}}, and the picture is filling in nicely. So far none of it pushes past the scope we set. A couple of the process timings do look longer than the summary you sent suggested, which is just the sort of thing the mapping is meant to surface, so we will want to check those with your team before we treat them as settled.

Two things would help us keep pace. We are still missing the expense-processing volumes for the last quarter of last year; our read of the Garcia-Werner numbers has a gap there that we would like to close with the actual figures before we build on it. And we would like to sit with one of your accounts-payable staff for an hour to watch a few invoices go through start to finish, so the map reflects how the work really runs and not only how the records describe it.

I will send the draft process map ahead of our next session, so we are all looking at the same thing when we meet. Let me know who I should arrange the accounts-payable hour with, and I will fit it around their schedule.
Pamela Miller
Working session minutes: back-office automation, Garcia-Werner
Session held to walk the current-state map to date, review the first list of automation candidates for {{fact:f:E-2021-001.client}}, and agree what still needs gathering before the diagnosis closes.
Present

items: Matthew Parrish, Research Associate, Calderwood Partners
Pamela Miller, Research Associate, Calderwood Partners
Mark Villegas, Chief Financial Officer, Garcia-Werner

blocks.text: Discussion

Pamela Miller took the group through the current-state map as it stands. Most of the transactional processes are now documented end to end: payables, receivables, the monthly reconciliations, and expense handling. Two are still partial, procurement paperwork and payroll administration, both waiting on records the client will send. The volume and effort figures are attached to each mapped process, drawn from the client's own reports where those exist and marked as provisional where they rest on a manager's estimate.

On method, the group confirmed what counts as automatable for this exercise. A step qualifies if it is repetitive, governed by rules that can be written down, and run on data already sitting in a system rather than on judgment applied case by case. Steps that fail any of those tests stay on the map but off the candidate list. Pamela Miller noted that a handful of steps sit on the line, where a rule exists but exceptions are frequent, and those are marked for a closer look rather than counted in or out yet.

The candidate list was reviewed line by line. The strongest candidates cluster where the work is high in volume and rule-based: invoice coding and matching, the routine month-end reconciliations, and expense-report checking. Each was tagged with a rough sense of the effort to automate and the time it would return, on the understanding that both firm up once the baseline is complete. Mark Villegas confirmed the ranking matched where his team feels the load day to day, and named invoice matching as the one he would most want to see move first.

Two gaps were recorded. The expense-processing volumes for the final quarter of last year are still missing, which leaves that estimate resting on three quarters rather than four. And the procurement process has one handoff nobody in the room could describe fully, between purchasing and accounts payable; it needs a short session with the person who runs it. Neither gap blocks the mapping, but both were noted so they are closed before the candidate list is called final.

The group also touched on how the second phase will read once it arrives. Each recommendation will carry three things: what the automated version of the process does, what it would take to stand up, and the time or cost it removes, measured back against the baseline agreed here rather than asserted. Mark Villegas asked that each recommendation also state plainly what stays manual, so his team can see where a person is still in the loop and why. That was agreed and will be built into the format. No decision on the recommendations themselves was taken today; the purpose of the session was to settle the map and the candidate list, and the ranking will not be called final until the two open gaps are closed.

On timing and access, Pamela Miller confirmed the next data request will be short, aimed only at the procurement and payroll records still outstanding, and will go to the routing contact rather than to several people at once. Mark Villegas confirmed those records exist and can be pulled this week.
Action items

rows: Complete the procurement and payroll maps once the records arrive
Firm up the effort and return estimates on the top candidates
Arrange a short session on the purchasing-to-payables handoff

blocks.text: Next session will review the completed map and the ranked candidate list. Recorded by Matthew Parrish.
Status report: back-office automation for Garcia-Werner
Prepared for the leadership of Garcia-Werner by Pamela Miller. This report covers the engagement to its midpoint.
Where things stand

The engagement is about halfway through. The first phase, mapping how the back-office work runs today and measuring what it costs in time and effort, is essentially complete and agreed with your team. We have moved into the second phase, which turns that picture into a ranked set of automation recommendations. Against the fixed fee of {{fact:f:E-2021-001.fee}}, which covers the whole engagement, the work is inside the scope and pace the engagement letter set, and we see nothing that would change either. In short, your team went over the current-state picture and it stood, it pointed clearly at where the routine work concentrates, and the recommendations now taking shape are aimed at those points.

One finding from the first phase is worth stating plainly, because the recommendations rest on it. The bulk of the manual effort in your back office sits in a handful of high-volume, rule-based processes, chiefly invoice coding and matching, the month-end reconciliations, and expense checking. These are also the processes where the work is most repetitive and the rules most consistent, which is what makes them the strongest candidates to automate. The rest of the back office is not being set aside, but it is either lower in volume or more a matter of judgment than of rule, and it earns a lower place in the sequence for that reason.
Completed since the last note

items: Finished the current-state map. Matthew Parrish closed out the last two process maps, procurement and payroll administration, once your records arrived, so every main back-office process at {{fact:f:E-2021-001.client}} is now documented end to end, with its volumes and effort attached and sourced to your own records.
Settled the effort baseline. The figures behind the map were reviewed with your team, and the few provisional numbers were either confirmed or set aside.

Ranked the automation candidates. With the baseline in place, each candidate was weighed against the effort to automate it and the time it would return, and the candidates were put into a working order for the recommendations.
Closed the open data gaps. The fourth-quarter expense volumes and the purchasing-to-payables handoff that were outstanding at the last working session are now in hand and folded into the baseline.

blocks.text: Fee and effort

The engagement is about half elapsed and, by our own reckoning, about half the effort is spent. Because the fee is fixed at {{fact:f:E-2021-001.fee}} rather than charged against hours, the question is not whether we have run over a rate but whether the work left to do fits the schedule and budget that remain, and it does. This note is only to confirm that, and that nothing in the second phase as it now looks would put it out.
The next few weeks

items: Turn the ranked candidates into a target design: for each recommended process, what the automated version does, what it needs to run, and what it leaves for a person to handle.
Estimate, from the agreed baseline, the time and cost each recommendation would return, so every claim in the briefing is a number your team can check.
Prepare the briefing for your wider group, to be given once the recommendations are firm enough to defend.
Set out a transition outline: the order to take the changes in, and what each one asks of your teams while it is put in place.

blocks.text: Risks and things to watch

items: Implementation is not in our scope. Our work ends with tested recommendations and a sequence; building and installing the automation is a separate step, whether your teams do it or a partner does. We flag it so the plan you receive is read for what it is.

Software selection. Where a recommendation points to a category of tool, we describe what the tool must do; choosing and negotiating a particular product is out of scope and would need its own decision on your side.

Change readiness. Automating a process changes how the people who run it spend their day. A sound recommendation can still stall if the affected staff are not brought along, and that part is shared between us and your managers.

Keeping the list focused. As the recommendations firm up there will be a pull to widen the list to processes that are lower in value or more a matter of judgment. We will flag each addition, but whether to reach beyond where the value concentrates is a call for your leadership as much as ours.

blocks.text: We are glad to walk through any of this in more detail whenever it suits you. The next set-piece is the briefing to your wider group, which we will schedule once the recommendations are ready to stand behind.
